Glossary About
Machine Minds

W. Ross Ashby

1903 – 1972

Regulation, variety, and mind as adaptive organization rather than inner essence.

The reconstruction I would defend is this: Ashby’s philosophy of mind is an anti-Cartesian, anti-homuncular, regulator-centered naturalism, but not a flat eliminative materialism. He treats the brain as an adaptive organ whose central function is the regulation of an organism’s essential variables in a changing environment. “Mind” is not an inner substance, a ghostly faculty, or a private theatre; it is the organized, embodied, self-stabilizing, information-using activity of a living system in relation to its world. Yet he also repeatedly marks the subjective datum of awareness as a serious philosophical residue: not something to be brushed aside, but something that exposes the incompleteness of ordinary physico-chemical description. That is why the strongest Ashbyan philosophy of mind is neither dualism nor reductive materialism, but a cybernetic functionalism with a phenomenological remainder.

1. The source situation

Ashby did not write a systematic treatise called “Philosophy of Mind.” The reconstruction has to be drawn from several layers of work: Design for a Brain; An Introduction to Cybernetics; the 1970 Conant–Ashby “good regulator” theorem; the journal and its indexes; the aphorisms; and later historical-philosophical interpretation. The digital archive is unusually important here because Ashby’s private journal ran from 1928 to 1972, with thousands of pages and an indexed structure that makes otherwise hidden themes visible. The archive’s own description emphasizes Ashby as a pioneer of cybernetics and systems theory, best known for the Law of Requisite Variety, Design for a Brain, An Introduction to Cybernetics, and the Homeostat.

The first methodological warning is that “soul” is not a central technical word in Ashby’s indexed corpus. Searches of the journal index do not show “Soul,” “God,” “Religion,” or “Theology” as organizing index entries, whereas “Conscious mind,” “Mind,” “Self-awareness,” “Subjective,” “Materialism,” “Introspection,” “Solipsism,” “Unconscious,” and related terms do appear. That absence should not be read as proof that theological or spiritual questions are irrelevant; it means rather that they enter Ashby’s thought obliquely, through psychical research, anti-Christian remarks, aphorisms about God, and his intense concern with consciousness and subjectivity.

2. The basic ontology: mechanism without crude mechanism

Ashby’s starting point is a bold mechanistic hypothesis. In Design for a Brain, he frames the central problem as follows: the nervous system appears machine-like in its physiological details, yet the organism produces adaptive, purposeful, intelligent behavior. His project is to show that these are not incompatible. He explicitly treats the nervous system as “essentially mechanistic” while asking what properties a system must have if it is to behave both mechanistically and adaptively.

But “mechanistic” does not mean “like a clock” or “like a trivial automaton.” In Ashby’s 1947 paper on self-organizing dynamic systems, a machine is a real dynamic system whose variables can be specified and whose subsequent behavior is determined by its configuration; the point of the paper is precisely to show how a determinate system can still exhibit apparent self-induced changes of organization. His ontology is therefore not the nineteenth-century picture of fixed gears; it is a mathematical ontology of variables, transformations, constraints, step-functions, stability, and reorganization.

This matters for mind. Ashby’s mechanistic mind is not a dead mechanism; it is a mechanism whose organization can change in response to the failure of behavior. What earlier traditions might have attributed to “soul,” “vital force,” “will,” or “inner purpose,” Ashby tries to explain by the dynamics of regulation, selection, and ultrastability. In Design for a Brain, he assumes that the living organism is not essentially different from other matter, while also insisting that adaptive behavior requires a more subtle theory of machine organization than ordinary physical intuition supplies.

The complication is that Ashby later refuses to let mechanism collapse into complacent materialism. A 1968 journal entry is indexed under “Conscious mind proves physics insufficient” and “Materialism inadequate.” The entry is terse but philosophically important: Ashby’s mature position seems to be that ordinary physico-chemical materialism lacks resources for the bare fact of awareness. This is not a clean conversion to dualism; it is a recognition that the scientific description of systems and variables has a limit when confronted with subjective awareness itself.

3. Systems, observers, and black boxes

Ashby’s philosophy of mind depends on his philosophy of systems. A “system” is not simply a thing waiting in the world with its boundaries already metaphysically fixed. It is an object considered under a chosen set of variables. In Design for a Brain, he defines the field of a system by observed variables and operations, and in An Introduction to Cybernetics he develops the black-box method: one studies the relation between input and output when the internal constitution is unknown or too complex to inspect directly.

This produces a strongly operational epistemology. Ashby’s “Other Index” under “Epistemology” includes formulations such as “knowledge must mean control if it is to be objective,” “all knowledge is knowledge of transformation,” and “knowing” as being in the right memory-state. In the “Subjective” section, he distinguishes raw feelings from the higher-order relations that science can communicate. This is one of the deepest points for consciousness: science is not denied, but science works by extracting communicable relations from a field whose primary givenness is subjective.

This gives us a first Ashbyan thesis: mind is not known by peering into an inner essence. Mind is inferred, modeled, and regulated through transformations. We know a mind, including our own, as a black-box system whose variables, outputs, constraints, and regulatory successes can be specified. That does not abolish subjectivity; it relocates it. Subjectivity is the primary experiential field, but objective knowledge begins only when relations among experiences become communicable, repeatable, and transformable.

4. Mind as regulation of essential variables

The center of Ashby’s mature theory is the concept of essential variables. In Design for a Brain, essential variables are those physiological variables closely linked to survival: pulse, respiration, temperature, and the like. Adaptation is then defined as the maintenance of these essential variables within proper limits. This is the decisive shift: mind is not first a contemplative faculty; it is an adaptive regulatory organization that keeps the organism viable.

Ashby explicitly equates adaptive behavior with stable-system behavior, provided that the stable region is one in which essential variables remain within normal limits. He also shifts the center of interest from movement and overt change to the constancy secured through movement: the organism may act vigorously, but the biological point of that activity is the preservation of crucial variables.

This is why the brain, for Ashby, is fundamentally a regulator. His aphorisms say that the function of the brain can be summed up as error-correction, that “to think is to act inside the brain,” and that behavior, functionally considered, is the brain. He repeatedly attacks the idea of a hidden inner executive: the brain has no brain inside it to guide it; it acts, transmits, monitors, and corrects.

Spanish-language reception captured this point early. A 1965 review of the Spanish translation Proyecto para un cerebro described the Homeostat not as a calculating machine but as a simulation of the brain considered as an organ specialized for organismic survival, correcting deviations of essential physiological variables caused by environmental perturbations. That is a very faithful interpretation: Ashby’s brain is not primarily a mirror of the world; it is a viability-preserving regulator.

5. Ultrastability: the cybernetic substitute for inner purpose

Ashby’s Homeostat is philosophically important because it dramatizes how a system can appear purposive without possessing an inner soul, intention, or conscious goal. When a system’s ordinary feedback loops fail and essential variables approach dangerous limits, a second-order mechanism changes the system’s parameters until a stable organization is found. This is ultrastability.

The point is not merely engineering. It is an ontology of adaptive life. The organism does not need a metaphysical purpose-substance; it needs a structure in which failed behavior triggers reorganization. Ashby’s trial-and-error machinery allows purpose-like behavior to be generated by lawful processes. Andrew Pickering describes the Homeostat as a major milestone in Ashby’s long attempt to understand the brain as a machine, and he notes that Wiener regarded it as one of the great philosophical contributions of the period.

The Homeostat therefore embodies an Ashbyan replacement for traditional teleology. Purpose is not abolished; it is reinterpreted as the maintenance of viable constraints through regulatory selection. What looks like goal-directedness is the system’s tendency to preserve essential variables by changing its own organization when necessary.

6. Perception, action, and the environment

Ashby’s mind is not skull-bound. In Design for a Brain, he stresses the two-way coupling of receptors, muscles, nervous system, and environment: receptors affect muscles through the nervous system, and muscles affect receptors through the environment. This is a sensorimotor loop, not a one-way input-to-representation pipeline.

His famous engine-driver example is anti-homuncular. The neurons of a driver do not need to “form a conception” of red, green, trains, signals, or accidents in order for the whole organism-machine system to respond correctly. The system’s parts can be locally blind while the whole acts intelligently. This is one of Ashby’s most important contributions to philosophy of mind: semantic-looking behavior can emerge from non-semantic component operations when the whole system is organized correctly.

This also explains why Ashby is close to later embodied and enactive theories of mind. Peter Asaro argues that Ashby’s later work, especially from 1967 to 1972, developed a conception of embodied mind relevant to situated robotics, ecological perception, and mechanisms of consciousness. Asaro’s reading is not an anachronistic imposition: it draws out what is already present in Ashby’s regulator-centered view of brain, body, and world.

Spanish-language scholarship has made the same point in a different vocabulary. Venturelli’s study of Ashby and the Macy conferences argues that Ashby’s Homeostat moves beyond simple behaviorism because the “world” of the Homeostat is itself dynamic, complex, and unpredictable: a dynamic system explores a dynamic world. Venturelli also highlights Ashby’s insistence, at the 1952 Macy discussion, that there cannot be a proper theory of the brain without a proper theory of the environment.

7. Representation: every good regulator must model

Ashby is not an eliminativist about representation. He rejects inner pictures and homunculi, but he does not reject models. The Conant–Ashby theorem says that, under broad conditions, the best simple regulator of a system must be a model of that system: its regulatory actions must map the relevant events of the regulated system. The theorem changes model-making from optional to compulsory in successful regulation.

This is the basis for an Ashbyan theory of mental representation. A mental representation is not primarily an image in consciousness or a sentence in a language of thought. It is a regulatory mapping that lets the organism reduce the variety of outcomes under disturbance. A model is “mental” when it is embedded in the organism’s control architecture and contributes to adaptive selection. The brain models its environment not because it contains a spectator looking at pictures, but because any competent regulator must embody mappings relevant to what it regulates.

This also clarifies Ashby’s relation to classical computationalism. He is not saying that the brain is a digital symbol manipulator. His core model is analogue, regulatory, and embodied. He is interested in stability, disturbance, thresholds, feedback, and viable action. The German Suhrkamp presentation of Einführung in die Kybernetik rightly emphasizes that the third part of the book applies mechanisms and information to the regulation and control of natural systems such as the human brain and to artificial machines comparable in function.

8. Intelligence as selection under variety

Ashby’s theory of intelligence follows directly from regulation. Intelligence is not an occult property, nor a special substance, nor even necessarily consciousness. It is appropriate selection from a set of possible actions or answers. Mick Ashby notes that Ross Ashby wrote in 1952 that intelligence is the ability to make an appropriate selection from a set of possible answers, and Ashby’s aphorisms similarly reduce “real intelligence” talk to performance: intelligent is as intelligent does.

The Law of Requisite Variety gives the formal limit: a regulator can reduce the variety of outcomes only by possessing or accessing sufficient variety of response. Ashby’s memorable formulation is that “variety can destroy variety.” In philosophy-of-mind terms, this means that intelligence is bounded by the repertoire of distinctions, actions, channels, and transformations available to the organism.

This gives a non-mystical but non-trivial theory of intelligence. A mind is intelligent to the degree that it can select appropriately under disturbance. It is not intelligent because it has an inner glow; it is intelligent because it can regulate outcomes in a world whose variety would otherwise overwhelm it. This is why Ashby can take machine intelligence seriously without asking whether the machine has an inner metaphysical essence.

9. Consciousness: not an executive, but not nothing

Ashby’s treatment of consciousness has two layers that must be kept apart.

The first layer is functional and cybernetic. Consciousness is not the inner ruler of the system. It is not a Cartesian theatre where a self watches mental images. Ashby’s aphorisms describe introspection as the output of a verbalizing mechanism and stress that having a brain does not mean knowing how one thinks. One can report mental events only insofar as they reach the mechanisms of report. That is a remarkably modern view: introspection is data, but it is not transparent access to the machinery of mind.

The second layer is phenomenal. Ashby does not simply explain consciousness away. His “Subjective” index distinguishes primary data in raw feelings from the higher relations that science can communicate. His 1968 consciousness note, indexed as showing physics insufficient and materialism inadequate, suggests that the fact of awareness itself remains a problem for ordinary scientific materialism.

The best reconstruction is therefore this: functional consciousness is reportable, monitored, verbally accessible regulatory information; phenomenal consciousness is the raw subjective givenness that scientific relations presuppose but do not fully capture. Ashby gives much more machinery for the first than for the second. But he is too honest to pretend that the second disappears.

His theological aphorism about God monitoring creation is relevant here. He writes that just as God has to monitor his actions in Genesis, so must the brain monitor its actions. This is not orthodox theology; it is a cybernetic reading of theology. Even divine creation is reimagined as feedback: action followed by evaluation. The point is that consciousness, insofar as it is a monitoring function, belongs to the logic of regulation rather than to a supernatural theatre.

10. Self-awareness and the impossibility of complete self-knowledge

Ashby’s journal repeatedly indexes “Self-awareness impossible.” That phrase should not be misread as saying that no organism can ever be aware of itself in any ordinary sense. It means that complete self-representation is impossible for formal cybernetic reasons. A system cannot fully represent all its own possibilities because those possibilities outnumber its available variety.

This is one of Ashby’s deepest contributions to philosophy of self. The self cannot be both the whole system and a complete representation of the whole system within itself. A self-model must be partial, compressed, selective, delayed, or externally mediated. The mind can monitor aspects of itself, but it cannot contain an exhaustive live map of its own total possible behavior.

His dream notes reinforce this decentering. One 1966 journal page is summarized as: “‘I’, in a dream, is not necessarily the site of intellectual activity.” A 1971 page is summarized as “Me and my brain in dreams” and again indexed under the impossibility of self-awareness. The dream-self is not the sovereign thinker; it is a role or construct within a larger process.

Thus Ashby’s theory of self is anti-Cartesian in a strong sense. The “I” is not the owner-controller of the brain. It is a partial product of regulatory, verbal, memory, and monitoring mechanisms. It can be useful; it can report; it can stabilize conduct. But it is not the metaphysical center of mind.

11. Free will, agency, and ethics

Ashby’s aphorisms are severe about free will. He treats much free-will talk as conceptual fog; the brain “decides” nothing in the homuncular sense—it acts. This does not mean that organisms are inert puppets. It means that agency must be redefined as system-level regulation, selection, and viable action, not as the intervention of an inner uncaused chooser.

A properly Ashbyan account of agency is therefore compatibilist in a cybernetic rather than classical philosophical sense. An agent is free to the degree that it has regulatory variety, information, and flexible organization adequate to its disturbances. A system with more variety can preserve more of its essential variables under more conditions. “Freedom” becomes effective adaptive range, not metaphysical exemption from causality.

Ethics is more difficult. Ashby’s aphorisms say that a computer knows no ethics, only a set-goal. That is consistent with his whole framework: cybernetics can describe how goals are pursued and how variables are regulated, but it does not by itself justify which essential variables ought to matter. The theory of mind is powerful as a theory of adaptive control; it is thinner as a theory of moral normativity.

12. Soul: what Ashby denies, what he transforms, and what remains

If “soul” means an immaterial, immortal substance that explains thought, will, and consciousness from outside the causal order, Ashby gives us no reason to attribute such a doctrine to him. His biography records that he was not religious and wrote “AGNOSTIC” on his army application form; it also records his resolve to follow Darwin’s footsteps.

Yet it would be a mistake to say that soul-adjacent questions are irrelevant to him. His early journal index includes hypnosis, “universal mind,” metagnosis, clairvoyance, hallucination, a psychic medium, and Osty’s Law. These entries show an Ashby willing to consider phenomena associated with psychical research and spiritualist traditions, not simply to dismiss them as beneath scientific attention. The crucial point is that he treats such topics as phenomena to be indexed, analyzed, and potentially mechanized or operationalized.

Andrew Pickering’s account of cybernetics and spirituality is especially important here. Pickering reports that Ashby’s private notebooks contained spiritualist and anti-Christian remarks, including a turn toward “time worship.” In that context, Ashby’s rejection is not of all transcendence-like language, but of a personalized, anthropomorphic Christian image of God. The spiritual residue shifts toward time, process, adaptation, and nonhuman scales of becoming.

This makes Ashby more philosophically interesting than a simple secular materialist. His “spirituality,” where it appears, is cybernetic and processual. Time is the medium through which adaptation emerges; feedback is the structure through which action becomes corrected; regulation is the principle by which life persists. A theological Ashby would not be a doctrine of divine personhood, but something closer to a reverence for time, constraint, and adaptive becoming.

His “Nirvana” and “nirvanophilia” language should be treated carefully. The journal index associates nirvanophilia with stable equilibrium and also records that Ashby later judged it wrong. The biography quotes a late formulation in which perfect adaptation, intelligence, and equilibrium culminate in “Nirvana.” This is not evidence of a Buddhist doctrine of soul; it is evidence that Ashby used religiously resonant language for the limiting case of complete equilibrium.

So the Ashbyan replacement for soul is not a new soul-substance. It is the adaptive organization of a living system through time. If one insists on a “soul-equivalent” in Ashby, it would be the organism’s dynamic pattern of regulation: embodied, environmental, historical, self-stabilizing, and partially self-monitoring. But that is a philosophical reconstruction, not a doctrine he explicitly names “soul.”

13. The “Book of God” and Ashby’s theological epistemology

Two aphorisms deserve special attention. One says that we cannot read the “book of God,” only the observer’s digest. Another says that, as God monitors creation, the brain must monitor its actions. These are not throwaway lines. They compress Ashby’s epistemology and theology into images.

“The observer’s digest” means that knowledge is never God’s-eye access to total reality. We receive selected, compressed, observer-relative relations among variables. This resembles negative theology structurally, though not doctrinally: the whole is not directly readable; we work with finite projections. It also resembles cybernetic operationalism: the observer’s instruments and distinctions shape the system known.

The Genesis-monitoring aphorism makes God cybernetic. Creation is not merely command; it is action plus feedback. The brain, likewise, cannot act intelligently without monitoring the effects of its acts. The theological image is therefore absorbed into the general theory of regulation. God becomes an analogy for feedback, not an exception to it.

14. Non-English reception and why it matters

The non-English material I found does not reveal a hidden school that reads Ashby as a theologian of the soul. It does, however, strongly reinforce the embodied, systemic, and anti-dualist interpretation.

The Spanish reception is especially useful. The 1965 Diánoia review of Proyecto para un cerebro treats Ashby’s Homeostat as a model of the brain as survival-regulator, and Venturelli’s 2016 article frames the Homeostat as philosophically relevant for contemporary cognitive science, model-based epistemology, and a move beyond simple behaviorism.

A Mexican article on cybernetics and cognitive science explicitly connects Ashby’s ultrastability to the later Latin American contribution of autopoiesis in Maturana and Varela. It stresses the difference between cybernetic artifacts and living systems: in living beings, identity and behavior are intrinsically bound, and “being and doing” are interdependent. That is a useful corrective to Ashby: his theory of regulation is profound, but autopoiesis asks whether life requires self-production, not merely self-stabilization.

The German reception, at least in the Suhrkamp presentation, emphasizes Ashby’s Introduction as a standard work of cybernetics whose third part concerns natural systems like the human brain and artificial machines comparable in function. This supports the interpretation of Ashby as a thinker of functional comparability between organism and machine, rather than a defender of human mental exceptionalism.

A recent Russian lecture on the Homeostat reads Ashby’s radicalism in strikingly anti-dualist terms: no firm boundary between organism and environment; brain, body, hand, and even thought become elements of the environment; the brain does not “think” so much as act. It also connects Ashby’s philosophy to later structuralism, autopoiesis, and enactivism. This is not primary Ashby, but it is a valuable contemporary non-English interpretation because it sees the ontological radicalism of Ashby’s system-boundary problem.

A Japanese essay on the history of cybernetics similarly links Ashby and Wiener to the continuity of organism and environment, then connects that continuity to cyborg thought and the destabilization of dualisms such as body/machine, human/nonhuman, mind/body, nature/culture, and male/female. The same essay also notes a spiritual or mythic dimension in cybernetics. That is important because Ashby’s soul-question is best understood not as a doctrine of immaterial soul, but as part of cybernetics’ wider pressure on inherited boundaries.

15. Relation to later embodied and enactive theories

Ashby anticipates embodied cognition because cognition is not an internal symbol game; it is regulation through body-world coupling. He anticipates enactivism because perception and action are inseparable in adaptive loops. He anticipates ecological psychology because the environment is not a passive stimulus-source but part of the system to be theorized.

But Ashby is not identical with enactivism. Froese and Stewart argue that Ashby’s abstract general-systems framework misses autonomy and phenomenological requirements that later autopoietic theory tries to supply. That criticism is fair. Ashby gives us a theory of adaptive regulation, but not a fully developed theory of lived meaning, biological self-production, or first-person concern.

The most balanced view is that Ashby provides the control-theoretic skeleton of an embodied philosophy of mind, while enactivism and autopoiesis try to add the living flesh: autonomy, self-production, normativity, and lived significance. Ashby’s theory is indispensable but incomplete.

16. The extracted Ashbyan philosophy of mind

Here is the comprehensive philosophy of mind that can be extracted from Ashby.

First, mind is not a substance. It is the organized activity of an adaptive system maintaining essential variables under disturbance. The brain is not the seat of a ghost; it is a regulator embedded in an organism-environment system.

Second, thought is action internal to a regulatory system. Thinking is not primarily contemplation; it is internal behavior that changes the system’s readiness to act, select, and stabilize. Concepts are stable invariants under operations, and knowledge is objective insofar as it becomes control of transformations.

Third, intelligence is appropriate selection. The intelligent system selects actions or answers from a range of possibilities in ways that preserve viability or achieve regulation. The degree of intelligence is constrained by available variety, information, and channel capacity.

Fourth, representation is regulatory modeling. A mind represents the world when it embodies mappings that let it regulate relevant disturbances. The model need not be conscious, pictorial, linguistic, or introspectively accessible.

Fifth, consciousness is split between monitoring and raw feel. As monitoring, consciousness is a functional feature of regulation: the system tracks its actions, errors, and reportable states. As raw feel, consciousness is the primary subjective datum that ordinary physics and communicable science do not fully capture.

Sixth, introspection is not sovereign knowledge. It is an output of a verbalizing mechanism. The subject reports what reaches report channels; it does not thereby know the full machinery that produced the report.

Seventh, the self is a partial self-model, not a homunculus. The “I” is not the site of all intellectual activity, as dreams reveal. Complete self-awareness is impossible because no system can fully represent all its own possibilities within itself.

Eighth, free will is replaced by regulatory agency. The relevant question is not whether an uncaused inner will intervenes, but how much variety, information, and adaptive reorganization a system has. Agency is effective control under disturbance.

Ninth, soul is not an explanatory substance, but Ashby leaves room for a transformed spiritual vocabulary. Time, adaptation, equilibrium, monitoring, and the unreadable whole of reality take over some roles traditionally assigned to soul or God. His theology is anti-anthropomorphic, cybernetic, and processual rather than ecclesial or doctrinal.

Tenth, human exceptionalism is weakened. Brains, organisms, machines, and environments are analyzed in the same vocabulary of systems, transformations, feedback, regulation, and variety. That does not prove humans are “nothing but machines” in the crude sense; it shows that machines, properly understood, are far richer than the old contrast allowed.

17. What Ashby leaves unresolved

Ashby leaves at least four major problems unresolved.

The first is the hard problem of consciousness. His 1968 note that awareness makes materialism inadequate is profound, but it is not a developed theory. He gives a cybernetics of monitoring and report, not a full metaphysics of phenomenal consciousness.

The second is biological autonomy. Ultrastability explains adaptive reorganization, but autopoiesis argues that living systems are not merely stable regulators; they produce and maintain their own identity. This is a genuine gap between Ashby and later enactive biology.

The third is normativity. Ashby can explain how a system preserves essential variables, but he does not fully explain why certain variables ought to count, especially in ethics, culture, and spiritual life. Cybernetics can formalize goal-seeking; it cannot by itself justify the goals.

The fourth is scale. Ashby’s Homeostat was philosophically brilliant, but scaling it into a realistic synthetic brain proved difficult. Pickering notes that Ashby ran into serious problems in moving beyond the Homeostat and that his synthetic-brain ambitions did not succeed as originally imagined.

Final assessment

Ashby’s philosophy of mind is best understood as a cybernetic metaphysics of adaptive organization. Mind is regulation; intelligence is appropriate selection; representation is a regulator’s model; thought is internal action; consciousness is both monitoring and an unresolved raw subjective datum; the self is a partial, non-sovereign model; and soul, if reconstructed at all, becomes the temporal organization of life maintaining itself through feedback and change.

He is therefore not a philosopher of the soul in the traditional theological sense. But he is absolutely relevant to the soul question, because he attacks the inherited assumptions that made “soul” seem necessary: the inner ruler, the immaterial will, the transparent self, the absolute mind/body boundary, and the human exception to the order of nature. At the same time, he does not trivialize consciousness. His late admission that awareness makes ordinary materialism inadequate prevents the system from closing too neatly.

The result is a philosophy of mind with unusual power: mechanistic but not mechanical, naturalistic but not complacently reductionist, anti-dualist but not blind to subjectivity, secular but haunted by theological and spiritual residues.

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
A personal research project by Fabián. · About · Glossary
